1: Psalm 134 is the final Song of Ascents (Pss 120-134), and it functions as the closing liturgical exchange of the pilgrimage festival. The collection of Songs of Ascents begins in the distant journey (Ps 120: "I dwell in Meshech") and ends here in the temple courts at night. "Servants of the LORD who minister in the house of the LORD by night (bĕlêlôt)" — the overnight Levitical guard rotations (1 Chr 9:27-33; 23:30). The Mishnah (Middot 1:1-2) describes the night watchmen's circuit of the temple courts. This final Song of Ascents thus closes the pilgrimage journey where it arrives: in the sanctuary itself, calling those who serve there through the night to bless YHWH even when the pilgrims have returned home.
2: "Lift up your hands (śĕʾû yĕdêkem) in the holy place and bless the LORD" — the gesture of uplifted hands was the standard posture for blessing and prayer in ancient Israel (Ps 28:2; 63:4; 141:2; 1 Kgs 8:22; Lam 2:19). "Holy place (qōḏeš)" — the sanctuary; the priests and Levites ministering in the temple courts lift their hands in the direction of the Most Holy Place. The call to bless YHWH reverses the typical direction of blessing: usually YHWH blesses Israel (as in the Aaronic benediction, Num 6:24-26). Here Israel — specifically the Levites on night duty — bless YHWH. The exchange of blessing is the pulse of covenant relationship.
3: "May the LORD bless you from Zion, he who made heaven and earth" — the divine blessing in response (v. 3 answers the human blessing of vv. 1-2). The Zion location of divine blessing connects the particular (Jerusalem temple) with the universal (maker of heaven and earth). This exact combination — "maker of heaven and earth" as the one who blesses "from Zion" — also appears in Ps 121:2 and 124:8. The cosmic creator is the local covenant God, and his local sanctuary is the point from which cosmic blessing radiates. The blessing formula closes the pilgrimage collection: those who have come to Jerusalem (Pss 120-134) receive the blessing they came for.
